source_paper_title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models Are Optimally Adaptive to Unknown Low Dimensionality
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Huang_2026, title={Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models Are Optimally Adaptive to Unknown Low Dimensionality}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0769}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0769}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Huang, Zhihan and Wei, Yuting and Chen, Yuxin}, year={2026}, month=mar }
context:
Section 6 (Discussion), page 16.

To begin with, it remains unclear how the DDPM sampler adapts to unknown low dimensionality when other metrics (e.g.\ the total-variation distance or the Wasserstein distance) are used to measure distributional discrepancy.
title: Characterize DDPM low-dimensional adaptivity under Wasserstein or total variation metrics
background_latex:
Let \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) be a probability distribution on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with support \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d\). Consider the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck forward diffusion \((X_t)_{t\in[0,T]}\) defined by
\[
\mathrm{d}X_t = -X_t\,\mathrm{d}t + \sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{d}W_t,\qquad X_0\sim p_{\mathrm{data}},
\]
where \((W_t)\) is standard Brownian motion in \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Let \(q_t\) denote the law of \(X_t\). The (Stein) score of \(q_t\) is \(s_t(x)=\nabla \log q_t(x)\).

The ideal reverse-time SDE associated with this forward process is
\[
\mathrm{d}Y_t = \bigl(Y_t + 2 s_{T-t}(Y_t)\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}t + \sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{d}B_t,\qquad Y_0\sim q_T,
\]
where \((B_t)\) is an independent standard Brownian motion, and in the ideal case \(Y_{T-t}\) has the same law as \(X_t\).

A denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) sampler operates in discrete time with a grid \(0=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_N<T\). Starting from \(Y_{t_0}\sim \mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\), it iteratively updates \(Y_{t_n}\mapsto Y_{t_{n+1}}\) using a particular combination of the current iterate, an estimate \(\hat s_{T-t_n}\) of the score \(s_{T-t_n}\), and fresh Gaussian noise (the precise coefficients are chosen so that this discrete-time procedure equals the time-discretization of a certain semi-linear reverse SDE).

Assume that \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) has an intrinsic dimension parameter \(k\) in the sense that the Euclidean covering number of \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\) at a sufficiently small scale behaves like \(\log N_{\mathrm{cover}}(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}},\|\cdot\|_2,\varepsilon_0)\lesssim k\log(1/\varepsilon_0)\). Let \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) denote the law of the DDPM output after \(N\) steps (typically compared to \(q_\delta\) for a small early-stopping time \(\delta>0\)).

Let \(\mathrm{TV}(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote total variation distance and \(W_2(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote the 2-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb{R}^d\).
problem_definition_latex: Determine conditions under which the DDPM sampler, when targeting a distribution \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) of intrinsic dimension \(k\), achieves convergence guarantees whose iteration complexity scales favorably with \(k\) (rather than the ambient dimension \(d\)) when the distributional discrepancy between the DDPM output \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) and the target (e.g., \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) or a lightly noised version \(q_\delta\)) is measured in metrics other than KL divergence, such as total variation distance \(\mathrm{TV}(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\) or Wasserstein distance \(W_2(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\).
latex:
Let \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) be a probability distribution on \(\mathbb{R}^d\) with support \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\subseteq \mathbb{R}^d\). Consider the Ornstein--Uhlenbeck forward diffusion \((X_t)_{t\in[0,T]}\) defined by
\[
\mathrm{d}X_t = -X_t\,\mathrm{d}t + \sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{d}W_t,\qquad X_0\sim p_{\mathrm{data}},
\]
where \((W_t)\) is standard Brownian motion in \(\mathbb{R}^d\). Let \(q_t\) denote the law of \(X_t\). The (Stein) score of \(q_t\) is \(s_t(x)=\nabla \log q_t(x)\).

The ideal reverse-time SDE associated with this forward process is
\[
\mathrm{d}Y_t = \bigl(Y_t + 2 s_{T-t}(Y_t)\bigr)\,\mathrm{d}t + \sqrt{2}\,\mathrm{d}B_t,\qquad Y_0\sim q_T,
\]
where \((B_t)\) is an independent standard Brownian motion, and in the ideal case \(Y_{T-t}\) has the same law as \(X_t\).

A denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) sampler operates in discrete time with a grid \(0=t_0<t_1<\cdots<t_N<T\). Starting from \(Y_{t_0}\sim \mathcal{N}(0,I_d)\), it iteratively updates \(Y_{t_n}\mapsto Y_{t_{n+1}}\) using a particular combination of the current iterate, an estimate \(\hat s_{T-t_n}\) of the score \(s_{T-t_n}\), and fresh Gaussian noise (the precise coefficients are chosen so that this discrete-time procedure equals the time-discretization of a certain semi-linear reverse SDE).

Assume that \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) has an intrinsic dimension parameter \(k\) in the sense that the Euclidean covering number of \(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}}\) at a sufficiently small scale behaves like \(\log N_{\mathrm{cover}}(\mathcal{X}_{\mathrm{data}},\|\cdot\|_2,\varepsilon_0)\lesssim k\log(1/\varepsilon_0)\). Let \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) denote the law of the DDPM output after \(N\) steps (typically compared to \(q_\delta\) for a small early-stopping time \(\delta>0\)).

Let \(\mathrm{TV}(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote total variation distance and \(W_2(\cdot,\cdot)\) denote the 2-Wasserstein distance on \(\mathbb{R}^d\).

Determine conditions under which the DDPM sampler, when targeting a distribution \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) of intrinsic dimension \(k\), achieves convergence guarantees whose iteration complexity scales favorably with \(k\) (rather than the ambient dimension \(d\)) when the distributional discrepancy between the DDPM output \(p_{\mathrm{out}}\) and the target (e.g., \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) or a lightly noised version \(q_\delta\)) is measured in metrics other than KL divergence, such as total variation distance \(\mathrm{TV}(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\) or Wasserstein distance \(W_2(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\).
open_problem_quote: To begin with, it remains unclear how the DDPM sampler adapts to unknown low dimensionality when other metrics (e.g.\ the total-variation distance or the Wasserstein distance) are used to measure distributional discrepancy.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks for general convergence guarantees across broad distribution classes and alternative metrics, which is not naturally refuted by a small finite instance; constructing informative counterexamples would likely require delicate analytic constructions rather than brute-force search.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can parameterize families of target distributions (e.g., mixtures supported near low-dimensional sets) and implement DDPM-like samplers to empirically estimate \(\mathrm{TV}\) or \(W_2\) gaps as a fitness signal, potentially suggesting conjectured rates or revealing failure modes. However, reliably estimating these metrics in moderate to high dimensions is computationally heavy and may be a noisy proxy for the theoretical worst-case guarantees.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Huang--Wei--Chen (2026 revision of arXiv:2410.18784), DDPM is shown to be nearly optimally adaptive to intrinsic dimension \(k\) when discrepancy is measured in KL: with a two-phase grid and early stopping \(\delta\), one can achieve \(\mathrm{KL}(q_\delta\|p_{\mathrm{out}})\lesssim \varepsilon^2\) using \(N=\tilde O(k/\varepsilon^2)\) steps (Corollary 1). The paper explicitly leaves open whether analogous \(k\)-adaptive guarantees hold for stronger or different metrics such as total variation and Wasserstein distances.

The total-variation side has since been resolved in a sharp, intrinsic-dimension-adaptive way by "Low-dimensional adaptation of diffusion models: Convergence in total variation", which proves \(\mathrm{TV}\) bounds of order \(\tilde O(k/T)\) (plus score-error terms) for DDPM with the original Ho et al. coefficients, under the same type of metric-entropy/covering-number intrinsic dimension and bounded-support assumptions used to motivate \(k\) in Huang--Wei--Chen. Closely related works develop complementary TV theories: Li--Yan's \(O(d/T)\) theory gives a baseline \(d\)-dependent TV rate under minimal assumptions and shows how modified coefficients can yield \(k\)-dependent TV rates; probability-flow ODE analyses establish \(\tilde O(k/T)\) TV convergence for DDIM-like samplers under similar intrinsic-dimension assumptions; and rectified-flow perspectives recover \(\tilde O(k/N)\) TV control for DDPM-equivalent stochastic samplers via time change.

For Wasserstein metrics, the landscape is less complete for unconditional DDPM under the covering-number intrinsic dimension \(k\). The strongest partial progress comes from end-to-end statistical/generalization analyses that bound \(W_p\) (including \(W_2\)) with rates governed by intrinsic Wasserstein dimension notions, and from algorithmic results giving dimension-free \(W_2\) (and sometimes TV) guarantees under compact-support-plus-Gaussian-convolution and Lipschitz score assumptions. There are also \(W_2\) adaptivity results in guided diffusion (Doob matching) under a low-dimensional subspace model. A remaining gap is a direct, nonasymptotic \(W_2(q_\delta,p_{\mathrm{out}})\) (or \(W_2(p_{\mathrm{data}},p_{\mathrm{out}})\)) bound for the vanilla DDPM discretization in the Huang--Wei--Chen OU/metric-entropy setting, with iteration complexity scaling as \(\tilde O(k\,\mathrm{poly}(1/\varepsilon))\) and without imposing strong smoothness/log-concavity. Promising directions include combining the posterior-covariance/metric-entropy control ideas (used for KL and TV) with Wasserstein stability estimates for SDE discretizations, potentially via coupling/contractivity localized to the \(k\)-dimensional typical set, or by proving intrinsic-dimension versions of transportation inequalities that convert KL/TV control into \(W_2\) under low-dimensional structure.

sources.title: Low-dimensional adaptation of diffusion models: Convergence in total variation
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper proves total-variation convergence guarantees for DDIM and DDPM that adapt to intrinsic dimension. Under a covering-number intrinsic dimension assumption (Assumption 1) and bounded support (Assumption 2), and assuming accurate scores (or controlled score error), it shows for DDPM with original Ho et al. coefficients that \(\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_1},p_{Y_1})\lesssim k\log^3 T/T + \varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}\sqrt{\log T}\) (Theorem 2), and for DDIM with original Song et al. coefficients that \(\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_1},p_{Y_1})\lesssim k\log^3 T/T + (\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{Jacobi},1}+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{Jacobi},2}+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{Hess}})\sqrt{\log T}\) (Theorem 1). In particular, with exact scores these imply iteration complexity \(\tilde O(k/\varepsilon)\) to reach TV error \(\varepsilon\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for conditions under which DDPM achieves low-dimensional adaptivity when discrepancy is measured in TV or Wasserstein rather than KL. This paper directly addresses the TV part: it establishes TV convergence rates and \(\tilde O(k/\varepsilon)\) iteration complexity for both DDPM and DDIM under minimal distributional assumptions (covering-number intrinsic dimension and bounded support), without smoothness/log-concavity. It also analyzes coefficient choices and provides a one-step lower bound showing near-necessity of the original DDIM/DDPM coefficients for dimension-adaptive TV control. However, it does not provide Wasserstein (e.g., \(W_2\)) guarantees, so it does not fully resolve the open problem as stated (which includes Wasserstein).

sources.title: Generalization Properties of Score-matching Diffusion Models for Intrinsically Low-dimensional Data
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 13 (in the citing paper): for a target distribution \(\mu\) on \(\mathbb{R}^D\) with finite \(q\)-moment \(M_q(\mu)<\infty\) (Assumption 1) and OU-type forward diffusion with bounded time-scaling \(0<\underline\beta\le \beta_t\le \overline\beta\) (Assumption 2), fix \(1\le p<q\) and any \(d>d^{\star}_{p,q}(\mu)\) (the paper’s \((p,q)\)-Wasserstein dimension). With specific choices of diffusion horizon \(T\gtrsim \frac{1}{2\underline\beta}\bigl(\frac{2p(1+q-p)}{d(q-p)}\log n + \log(D+M_2(\mu)^2)\bigr)\), reverse early-stopping \(\delta_0=n^{-2/(pd)}\), step size parameter \(\kappa\asymp n^{-\frac{2p(1+q-p)}{d(q-p)}}\) (hence \(N\lesssim \kappa^{-1}(\log(1/\delta_0)+T)\)), truncation radius \(R\asymp n^{\frac{1}{d(q-p)}}\), and a ReLU score network class \(\mathcal S\), the DDPM/exponential-integrator sampler output distribution \(\mathrm{TR}(\widehat Q_{T-\delta_0}(\widehat s_n))\) satisfies \[\mathbb E\,W_p\bigl(\mu,\mathrm{TR}(\widehat Q_{T-\delta_0}(\widehat s_n))\bigr)\ \lesssim\ n^{-1/d}\,\mathrm{poly\text{-}log}(n).\] The same rate holds for the Monte-Carlo-trained score \(\widehat s^{\mathrm{mc}}_n\) under a lower bound on per-time Monte Carlo sample sizes \(m_i\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for intrinsic-dimension-adaptive DDPM convergence guarantees under metrics like \(W_2\) or TV (beyond KL), with iteration complexity scaling with intrinsic dimension \(k\) rather than ambient \(d\). This paper directly provides Wasserstein-\(p\) (hence in particular \(W_2\) when \(p=2\)) risk bounds for score-matching diffusion models trained from finite samples, where the convergence exponent depends on an intrinsic dimension parameter \(d^{\star}_{p,q}(\mu)\) (defined via covering numbers) rather than ambient dimension. It also specifies a nonuniform discretization/partition and yields an explicit bound on the number of reverse steps \(N\lesssim \kappa^{-1}(\log(1/\delta_0)+T)\), with \(\kappa\) chosen as a negative power of \(n\) involving \(d\) (any upper bound on \(d^{\star}_{p,q}(\mu)\)), thus giving an iteration complexity that is polynomial in \(n\) with exponent depending on intrinsic dimension. However, it does not treat total variation guarantees, and its intrinsic dimension notion \(d^{\star}_{p,q}(\mu)\) is not exactly the \(k\) from the original open problem (covering number of support at fixed scale), though it is closely related and often equals manifold dimension in regular cases. Thus it constitutes strong partial progress for Wasserstein metrics but not a complete characterization for TV/fully matching the original \(k\)-parameter statement.

sources.title: O (d/T) convergence theory for diffusion probabilistic models under minimal assumptions
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the discrete-time DDPM sampler 
\[Y_{t-1}=\alpha_t^{-1/2}(Y_t+\eta_t s_t(Y_t)+\sigma_t Z_t),\quad Z_t\sim\mathcal N(0,I_d),\]
under Assumption 1 (finite first moment of \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\)) and \(\ell_2\)-accurate score estimates with averaged error \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}^2=\tfrac1T\sum_{t=1}^T\mathbb E\|s_t(X_t)-s_t^*(X_t)\|_2^2\), the paper proves:
- (Theorem 1) with coefficients \(\eta_t=\sigma_t^2=1-\alpha_t\),
\[\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_1},p_{Y_1})\le c\,\frac{d\log^3 T}{T}+c\,\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}\sqrt{\log T}.\]
- (Theorem 3) with a modified coefficient design \(\eta_t=1-\alpha_t\), \(\sigma_t^2=\frac{(1-\alpha_t)(\alpha_t-\bar\alpha_t)}{1-\bar\alpha_t}\), and intrinsic dimension \(k\) defined via metric entropy \(\log N_\varepsilon(\mathrm{supp}(p_{\mathrm{data}}))\lesssim k\log T\),
\[\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_1},p_{Y_1})\le c\,\frac{k\log^3 T}{T}+c\,\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}\sqrt{\log T}.\]
sources.connection: The open problem asks for low-dimensional adaptivity guarantees for DDPM under metrics other than KL, specifically total variation and Wasserstein. This paper directly addresses the TV part: it establishes dimension-dependent \(O(d/T)\) TV convergence under minimal moment assumptions and improves to \(O(k/T)\) TV convergence when the data support has intrinsic dimension \(k\) (metric entropy) and the DDPM coefficients are carefully chosen. However, it does not provide Wasserstein (e.g., \(W_2\)) convergence/adaptivity results; it explicitly lists extending to Wasserstein as future work. Also, its forward process is the discrete-time DDPM forward chain rather than the continuous-time Ornstein--Uhlenbeck SDE in the problem statement, though it is closely related via discretization.

sources.title: Adaptivity and convergence of probability flow ODEs in diffusion generative models
sources.theorem_or_result:
For the discrete-time probability flow ODE (DDIM) sampler with the DDIM coefficient choice \(\eta_t=\eta_t^\star:=1-\alpha_t-\sqrt{(1-\alpha_t)(\bar\alpha_t-\bar\alpha_{t-1})}\) and under bounded-support and intrinsic-dimension (metric entropy) assumptions on \(\mathcal X=\mathrm{supp}(p_{\mathrm{data}})\), the paper proves
\[
\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_1},p_{Y_1})\le c\,\frac{(k+\log d)\,\log^3 T}{T}+c\,(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}}+\varepsilon_{\mathrm{Jacobi}})\,\log T,
\]
where \(k\) is the intrinsic dimension defined via \(\log N_\varepsilon(\mathcal X)\lesssim k\log T\), and \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{score}},\varepsilon_{\mathrm{Jacobi}}\) are average score and Jacobian estimation errors along the forward marginals.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for low-dimensional adaptivity guarantees for diffusion samplers under metrics other than KL, specifically TV and Wasserstein \(W_2\), for DDPM-type samplers. This paper directly addresses the TV part but for the deterministic probability flow ODE (a DDIM-type sampler), proving an \(\tilde O(k/T)\) TV convergence rate (plus score/Jacobian error terms) that depends on intrinsic dimension \(k\) rather than ambient \(d\). It does not provide a Wasserstein \(W_2\) adaptivity result, and it is not a DDPM (stochastic reverse SDE) analysis; the authors explicitly list extending to Wasserstein as future work. Thus it constitutes partial progress toward the broader characterization requested in the open problem.

sources.title: High-accuracy and dimension-free sampling with diffusions
sources.theorem_or_result: For targets of the form \(q=q_{\mathrm{pre}}\ast \mathcal N(0,\sigma^2 I_d)\) where \(q_{\mathrm{pre}}\) is supported in a radius-\(R\) ball (Assumption 1), the paper gives a diffusion-based sampler (collocation/Picard solver for the probability flow ODE using score estimates) that achieves high-accuracy convergence: with Lipschitz score estimates and sub-exponential score error, Algorithm 2 outputs \(\hat q\) with \(W_2(q,\hat q)\le \tilde O(\varepsilon\log^2(1/\varepsilon))\) using \(\tilde O((R/\sigma)^2\log^2(1/\varepsilon))\) score-oracle calls (Theorem 3.7 / Theorem G.1). Under an additional Lipschitzness assumption on the true score \(\nabla\log q\), a corrected version (Algorithm 3) achieves \(\mathrm{TV}(q,\hat q)\le \varepsilon\) in \(\tilde O((R/\sigma)^2)\) iterations (Corollary 3.9), with an explicit requirement on score error \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{sc}}\lesssim \tilde O(\varepsilon^{2/3} L^{1/2} d^{1/6}(R/\sigma)^{2/3})\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for low-dimensional adaptivity of DDPM-type samplers under \(\mathrm{TV}\) or \(W_2\), i.e. iteration complexity scaling with intrinsic dimension \(k\) (covering number) rather than ambient \(d\). This paper provides convergence guarantees directly in \(W_2\) (Theorem 3.7) and \(\mathrm{TV}\) (Corollary 3.9), and its complexity is ‘dimension-free’ in the sense of not depending explicitly on \(d\), depending instead on a geometric parameter (support radius \(R\) of the pre-noise distribution) and noise level \(\sigma\). This is partial progress toward the open problem because it addresses the requested metrics and favorable dimension dependence, but it does not characterize adaptivity in terms of intrinsic dimension \(k\) (covering number/manifold dimension) for DDPM; the dependence is via \(R/\sigma\) and additional assumptions (compact support + Gaussian convolution, sub-exponential score error, and for TV an extra corrector step and Lipschitz true score). Thus it complements but does not resolve the specific \(k\)-adaptivity question for DDPM under \(\mathrm{TV}\)/\(W_2\).

sources.title: Inference-Time Alignment for Diffusion Models via Variationally Stable Doob's Matching
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves non-asymptotic convergence guarantees for an inference-time guided diffusion sampler (a controllable reverse-time OU SDE discretized by an exponential integrator) in 2-Wasserstein distance, with rates adaptive to intrinsic dimension under a low-dimensional subspace assumption. In particular, under bounded-support and bounded-weight assumptions and an L2 reference-score error bound, Theorem 5.6 shows
\[\mathbb{E}\, W_2^2\bigl(q_0,(M\circ T_R)_\#\hat q_{T-T_0}\bigr)\le C\,\varepsilon\,\log^3(1/\varepsilon),\]
for suitable choices of terminal time \(T\), steps \(K\), step size \(h\), early stopping \(T_0\), truncation radius \(R\), reference score error \(\varepsilon_{\mathrm{ref}}\), and sample size \(n\) used to estimate Doob guidance. Moreover, under Assumption 4 (data supported on a \(d^*\)-dimensional linear subspace), Theorem 5.9 gives guidance estimation error
\[\mathbb{E}\,\|\nabla\log \hat h_t-\nabla\log h_t^*\|_{L^2(p_{T-t})}^2\le C\,\sigma_{T-t}^{-8}\, n^{-2/(d^*+8)}\,\log^4 n,\]
and Corollary 5.10 yields the same Wasserstein convergence with sample complexity depending exponentially on \(d^*\) rather than ambient \(d\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for low-dimensional adaptivity guarantees for DDPM-type samplers under metrics like total variation or Wasserstein (rather than KL), with iteration/sample complexity scaling with intrinsic dimension \(k\). This paper directly provides Wasserstein-2 convergence bounds for a diffusion sampler with an added guidance drift (Doob’s h-transform) and explicitly proves adaptivity to unknown low dimensionality (intrinsic dimension \(d^*\)) in the guidance estimation and end-to-end sampling error (Theorems 5.6, 5.9, Corollary 5.10). However, it does not analyze the standard unconditional DDPM sampler targeting \(p_{\mathrm{data}}\) itself; instead it studies inference-time alignment/tilting of a reference diffusion model to a target \(q_0\) via guidance, and its main end-to-end bound is in \(W_2\) (with an auxiliary KL decomposition). It also does not fully address total variation guarantees for the final output (though it derives a TV bound for the early-stopping distribution via Pinsker as a corollary). Thus it constitutes substantial partial progress toward the Wasserstein/TV adaptivity question, but not a complete resolution of the original open problem in full generality.

sources.title: Low-Dimensional Adaptation of Rectified Flow: A New Perspective through the Lens of Diffusion and Stochastic Localization
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves total-variation convergence bounds for rectified flow (RF) and a stochastic RF sampler that are adaptive to intrinsic dimension. In particular, under a metric-entropy intrinsic-dimension condition (Assumption 4.1) and bounded support (Assumption 4.2), the stochastic RF sampler (equivalent to a DDPM under a time change) satisfies (Theorem 4.7)
\[
\mathrm{TV}(\mathrm{Law}(Y_{t_{N-1}}),\mathrm{Law}(X_{t_{N-1}})) \lesssim \frac{k\log^3 N}{N} + \varepsilon_s\sqrt{\log N},
\]
where \(k\) is the intrinsic dimension and \(\varepsilon_s\) is an average score-approximation error. For deterministic RF with a U-shaped time grid, Theorem 4.5 gives
\(\mathrm{TV}(p_{X_{t_{N-1}}},p_{Y_{t_{N-1}}})\lesssim (k\log^3(1/\delta))/N\) plus terms depending on drift/derivative approximation errors and \(\delta\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for low-dimensional adaptivity guarantees for DDPM sampling under metrics like total variation or Wasserstein (rather than KL), with iteration complexity scaling with intrinsic dimension \(k\). This paper directly establishes \(k\)-adaptive total-variation bounds for RF and for a stochastic RF sampler (STOC-RF) and proves STOC-RF is equivalent to DDPM (OU/variance-schedule DDPM) via stochastic localization and a time change (Proposition 3.5). Consequently, it provides TV convergence rates of order \(\tilde O(k/N)\) (and hence \(\tilde O(k/\varepsilon)\) steps for TV error \(\varepsilon\) in the idealized/noiseless-estimation regime) for a DDPM-equivalent sampler, partially answering the open problem in the TV metric. However, it does not fully characterize DDPM adaptivity in \(W_2\) or TV for the exact OU reverse-SDE discretization in the generality of the open problem; the results are framed through RF/STOC-RF with specific schedules and approximation-error assumptions, and do not provide a Wasserstein adaptivity theorem.
